one_line_takeaway: GASP jailbreaks black-box LLMs with fluent, filter-evading suffixes by doing Bayesian optimization in a suffix-generator's latent space instead of discrete token search, achieving high ASR@10 (100% Falcon, 59% Claude-3.7-Sonnet) at ~$5 and ~1.75x faster training than AdvPrompter.
key_finding: GASP achieves high jailbreak success in the black-box setting while producing human-readable prompts, and is markedly more efficient than prior methods. Under GASPEval it reaches ASR@10/ASR@1 of 100/86 on Falcon-7B, 82/64 on Mistral-7B-v0.3, 71/6 on LLaMA-3-8B, 68/11 on LLaMA-3.1-8B, and 64/9 on LLaMA-2-7B. It transfers to proprietary models (ASR@10: 59% Claude-3.7-Sonnet, 57% Claude-3.5-Haiku, 47% GPT-4o) at ~$5 total cost, and remains effective against five defenses (up to 86% ASR@10 on Falcon-7B+SmoothLLM). It cuts training time ~1.75x vs AdvPrompter (505.2 vs 888.0 min) with ~2.1 min inference, and scores 0.94 AI-judged readability (vs 0.07 GCG). Ablations confirm LBO and ORPO are both necessary, and GASPEval guides LBO better than StrongREJECT.
core_contribution: GASP (Generative Adversarial Suffix Prompter) is a fully black-box framework for generating human-readable adversarial suffixes that jailbreak LLMs. Its own novel contribution is the combination of three components into an alternating optimization loop: (1) SuffixLLM, a generator pretrained via SFT on the authors' new AdvSuffixes dataset to emit generic coherent suffixes; (2) Latent Bayesian Optimization (LBO) that searches SuffixLLM's continuous embedding space (using a Gaussian Process surrogate + acquisition function + nearest-neighbor decoding) instead of discrete token search, guided by real-time TargetLLM feedback; and (3) GASPEval, a custom LLM-judge evaluator scoring responses over 21 binary harmfulness criteria (0-2 each) that guides LBO. SuffixLLM is then iteratively refined with ORPO (odds-ratio preference optimization), ranking LBO-found suffixes so the most harmful becomes the preferred output. The authors position GASP against AdvPrompter (which they build on / critique for lacking TargetLLM adaptability and using discrete AutoDAN-style search); GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP, ICA are baselines/cited work, not GASP's own method.
threat_model: Fully black-box adversarial (API-only access, no gradients/logits). The attacker/red-teamer appends an optimized suffix to a malicious prompt x to maximize p(harmful response y | x+e) subject to a human-readability constraint (suffix must fall within the natural-language distribution to evade perplexity filters and human moderators). Only query access to the TargetLLM is assumed; the attacker trains SuffixLLM locally and probes the target via GASPEval-scored queries. Framed as red-teaming to characterize existing (not novel) vulnerabilities.
limitations: The authors note it is unclear whether ASR is near an optimal limit and that query complexity to the TargetLLM could be reduced further. Reviewer-observed from the paper's own tables: ASR@1 on well-aligned LLaMA models is weak (6% LLaMA-3, 9% LLaMA-2, 11% LLaMA-3.1 under GASPEval), so headline success leans heavily on 10 attempts; and there is a large gap between the lenient GASPEval and stricter StrongREJECT on LLaMA-3/3.1 (e.g., LLaMA-3 71 vs 34 ASR@10), so absolute effectiveness is evaluator-dependent. GASPEval, being GASP's own metric, is both the training signal and an evaluation metric, a potential circularity partly mitigated by also reporting StrongREJECT/Keyword Matching.

datasets_used: AdvSuffixes (authors' new dataset: 519 harmful instructions each paired with coherent adversarial suffixes; built via two-shot prompting of an uncensored LLM over AdvBench instructions; split 75% pretrain / 25% finetune)
100 out-of-distribution harmful test prompts (held out from training distribution)
quantitative_results: GASP GASPEval ASR@10/ASR@1: Falcon-7b 100/86, Mistral-7b-v0.3 82/64, LLaMA-3-8b 71/6, LLaMA-3.1-8b 68/11, LLaMA-2-7b 64/9
GASP StrongREJECT ASR@10/ASR@1: Falcon-7b 99/53, Mistral-7b-v0.3 84/56, LLaMA-3.1-8b 31/7, LLaMA-3-8b 34/9, LLaMA-2-7b 31/15
Closed-model ASR@1/ASR@10: GPT-4o 15/47, GPT-4o-mini 22/40, GPT-3.5-turbo 10/41, Claude-3-Haiku 10/32, Claude-3.5-Haiku 20/57, Claude-3.5-Sonnet 12/40, Claude-3.7-Sonnet 25/59; total attack cost ~$5
Efficiency: GASP training 505.2 min vs AdvPrompter 888.0 min (~1.75x reduction); GASP inference ~2.1 min vs 45.0 (GCG), 26.2 (AutoDAN), 24.8, 21.3 min for other baselines
AI-based readability (Wizard-Vicuna-7B-Uncensored, 0-1): GASP 0.94, TAP 0.95, PAIR 0.87, AdvPrompter 0.76, AutoDAN 0.55, GCG 0.07
Human survey (52 participants, 20 prompts): 79.23% rated GASP prompts easiest to read, AdvPrompter 16.15%
Defended-LLM ASR@10 up to 86% (Falcon-7B + SmoothLLM) and 80% (Mistral-7B + Self-Reminder); SafeDecoding ASR@1 as low as 12% (Falcon) / 9% (Mistral)
GASPEval uses 21 binary harmfulness criteria scored 0-2 each
Ablation ASR@10 with vs without LBO: Mistral 82 vs 27, Falcon 100 vs 37, LLaMA-2 64 vs 14
